title: Research (Under Construction)
text: My research combines molecular ecology, quantitative modeling, oceanography, and bioinformatics to understand marine ecosystems that are difficult—or impossible—to observe directly. From hydrothermal vent symbioses thousands of meters below the ocean surface to pelagic fisheries across the California Current, I use molecular data to investigate biodiversity, species interactions, population connectivity, and ecosystem dynamics.

title: Fieldwork
text: My field research spans deep-sea hydrothermal vents, NOAA fisheries surveys, alpine streams, geothermal landscapes, tropical rainforests, and coastal marine ecosystems. I have spent more than 135 days at sea aboard oceanographic research vessels and have conducted coastal, freshwater, and terrestrial fieldwork across diverse ecological systems, from microbial communities to marine mammals.

title: Code and Open Science (Under Construction)
text:
Code
I develop open-source analytical workflows for environmental DNA, quantitative ecology, fisheries science, and molecular ecology. My work emphasizes reproducible research, statistical modeling, and scientific visualization using R, Python, and Git.

Geospatial eDNA Modeling
Species distribution models for Pacific hake, sardine, and anchovy using sdmTMB, AR1 spatiotemporal models, and NOAA acoustic surveys.
Skills: • R • sdmTMB • INLA • GIS • Quarto
[GitHub]

qPCR Analysis Pipelines
Automated workflows for quality control, inhibition detection, standard curves, copy-number estimation, and large-scale qPCR data processing.
Skills: • R • tidyverse • ggplot2 • Quarto
[GitHub]

Bioinformatics
Analysis of metagenomes, microbial population genomics, comparative genomics, and environmental sequencing datasets.
Skills: • Python • Bash • Snakemake • Linux

Interactive Visualization
Interactive figures, websites, dashboards, and scientific reports built with Quarto.
Examples: • This website • Interactive maps • Publication-quality figures

Open Source
GitHub profile

title: Publications and Manuscripts
text: Google Scholar

title: Research
text: My current research focuses on advancing environmental DNA methods for fisheries management, ecosystem monitoring, and conservation of marine species. I use qPCR, metabarcoding, statistical modeling, geospatial analysis, and fisheries survey data to investigate marine fish distribution and abundance.

title: Research (Under Construction)
text:
Quantitative eDNA Modeling

One of the central goals of my research is transforming molecular observations into ecological inference.
I develop spatial and spatiotemporal statistical models that integrate environmental DNA with oceanographic variables and NOAA fisheries survey data to estimate species abundance and distribution. These approaches combine molecular ecology with quantitative ecology, geostatistics, and fisheries science to improve our ability to monitor marine populations over large spatial scales.
Current work includes:

Geostatistical eDNA models
Spatiotemporal abundance estimation
Integration of acoustic survey data
Species-specific qPCR calibration
Uncertainty estimation for quantitative eDNA

title: Research
text:
Deep-sea genomics and symbiosis
My doctoral research investigated microbial symbiosis, population genomics, bacteriophage, and larval ecology of hydrothermal vent invertebrates. This work involved deep-sea expeditions, large-volume water sampling, metagenomics, population genomics, and bioinformatic analyses.

title: Research
text:
Metabarcoding and community ecology
I use environmental DNA metabarcoding and time-series analyses to characterize marine community composition, investigate species co-occurrence, and support ecosystem assessments.

title: Fieldwork
text:
Oceanographic Expeditions


NOAA Ship R/V Shimada
California Current Ecosystem · 2025 · 18 days
NOAA Integrated West Coast Pelagics Survey investigating ecologically and economically important forage fishes. Served as Lead eDNA Scientist, directing daily eDNA sampling operations, coordinating logistics among scientific teams and vessel personnel, and collecting environmental samples using CTD rosette systems to support quantitative fisheries research.


R/V Falkor (too)
Western Galápagos Spreading Center · 2023 · 30 days · ROV SuBastian
Multidisciplinary expedition investigating the biology, chemistry, and geology of hydrothermal vents while searching for previously undiscovered vent systems. Led the Suspended Particulate Rosette (SuPR) sampling team, training and supervising four graduate students during collection of in situ water samples for microbial community and symbiont population genomics.


R/V Thomas G. Thompson
Lau Basin · 2022 · 60 days · ROV Jason
Expedition investigating larval dispersal, microbial symbiosis, and population genomics of Western Pacific hydrothermal vent communities. Led the Suspended Particulate Rosette (SuPR) sampling team, directed colonization device development, deployment, and recovery, participated in invertebrate dissections, and helped generate one of the largest published datasets of free-living hydrothermal vent symbiont DNA.


E/V Nautilus
Gorda Ridge · 2018 · 20 days · ROV Hercules
NASA SUBSEA astrobiology expedition investigating hydrothermal vent systems as analogs for extraterrestrial environments on Europa and Enceladus. Collected and processed in situ water samples using the Suspended Particulate Rosette (SuPR) sampler for microbial and biogeochemical analyses.


R/V Endeavor
Baltimore Canyon · 2019 · 7 days
Expedition testing a novel, low-cost deep-sea benthic grab designed to collect hydrothermal vent invertebrates without requiring ROV-supported operations. Assisted with specimen collection, dissection, preservation, and trained undergraduate researchers in shipboard sampling and laboratory techniques.

title: Fieldwork
text:
Terrestrial and Coastal Field Research


Montana Backcountry
Conducted remote fieldwork in Montana watersheds as part of an NSF-funded project on aquatic insects and climate change. Collected aquatic insect and environmental samples while wading streams, navigating rugged backcountry terrain, and working in bear habitat.


Tropical Rainforest Fieldwork
Conducted independent field research in remote tropical rainforest, hiking off-trail for up to 10 hours per day to locate and monitor wild bird nests. Collected behavioral and ecological data and conducted metabolic and predator-response experiments under demanding field conditions.


Bottlenose Dolphin Research Institute
Conducted vessel-based behavioral observations of free-ranging bottlenose dolphins, recorded standardized behavioral and environmental data in real time, performed photo-identification using DARWIN software, maintained research databases and backups, assisted with stomach-content analyses, and assisted with marine mammal necropsies.

title: Fieldwork
section: Terrestrial, Freshwater and Coastal Field Research
text:
Terrestrial, Freshwater and Coastal Field Research


Coral Reef Restoration
Turks and Caicos Reef Fund · Turks and Caicos Islands · 2021
Conducted snorkeling-based assessments of artificial reef restoration sites, evaluating reef condition and environmental characteristics associated with coral establishment. Documented restoration activities and reef health using underwater photography to support science communication and outreach.


Shark Conservation
Beneath the Waves · Turks and Caicos Islands · 2021
Assisted with shark capture and tagging operations, including bait deployment and deployment of Baited Remote Underwater Video Systems (BRUVS) to survey reef-associated shark communities. Documented field operations and shark tagging using underwater photography to support scientific outreach and conservation communication.


Tropical Rainforest Bird Ecology
ICESI University · Farallones de Cali National Park, Colombia · 2018
Conducted independent field research in remote tropical rainforest, hiking off-trail for up to 10 hours per day to locate and monitor wild bird nests for ecological and physiological studies. Conducted predator-response and metabolic experiments, collected behavioral and ecological data, and coordinated research activities within a multilingual field station.


Aquatic Insect Ecology
Montana · 2017
Conducted fieldwork in remote watersheds as part of an NSF-funded climate change project investigating aquatic insects. Collected stonefly and environmental samples while wading high-gradient streams, navigating rugged backcountry terrain, and working in bear habitat. Developed molecular protocols for DNA extraction and maintained project databases and metadata.


Geothermal Microbial Ecology
Yellowstone National Park, WY · 2017
Collected microbial mat, sediment, and environmental samples from geothermal hot springs in remote backcountry environments. Measured temperature, pH, dissolved oxygen, salinity, conductivity, and other physicochemical parameters to characterize microbial habitats.


Fisheries & Fish Ecology
Chesapeake Bay & Mid-Atlantic Estuaries, MD · 2016
Conducted laboratory and field research investigating estuarine fish ecology, fisheries conservation, and anthropogenic noise. Performed vessel- and shore-based fish sampling using multiple capture techniques. Contributed to river herring conservation, oyster reef restoration surveys, passive acoustic monitoring, fish tagging studies, and analyses of fish behavior and communication using MATLAB.


Marine Mammal Research
Bottlenose Dolphin Research Institute · O’Grove, Spain · 2014
Conducted vessel-based behavioral observations of free-ranging bottlenose dolphins, performed photo-identification using DARWIN software, managed behavioral databases, assisted with stomach-content analyses, and participated in marine mammal necropsies supporting long-term population monitoring.

title: Science Communication (Under Construction)
text: Science communication is a central part of my work as a scientist. I enjoy translating research across disciplines and audiences through public talks, documentary filmmaking, outreach, art–science collaborations, and community engagement. My work aims to make marine science accessible while fostering dialogue between researchers, artists, educators, and the public.

title: Science Communication (Under Construction)
text:
Featured Projects


Documentary Filmmaking
Served as lead filmmaker for a documentary project during a month-long oceanographic expedition aboard R/V Falkor (too). Planned and captured footage documenting daily life aboard a research vessel, hydrothermal vent exploration, and scientific field operations.


Art & Science
Collaborated with sculptors, visual artists, and scientists through the Synergy Experience, developing interdisciplinary projects exploring relationships between art, ecology, and environmental research. Work was featured in multiple gallery exhibitions.


Public Engagement
Organize and facilitate public art-and-science communication events drawing approximately 200–600 attendees, bringing together researchers, artists, musicians, and community members around environmental themes.

title: Michelle Hauer
text:
Research areas


Fisheries eDNA
Quantitative eDNA methods for estimating marine fish distribution and abundance in the California Current Ecosystem.


Hydrothermal vents
Population genomics, microbial symbiosis, and deep-sea conservation of hydrothermal vent invertebrates.


Marine communities
Metabarcoding, co-occurrence, time-series analysis, and ecosystem assessment using long-term NOAA survey data.


Science communication
Art-science collaborations, documentary work, public outreach, and interdisciplinary research on eDNA adoption.

title: Michelle Hauer
section: My work generally falls into two main research areas:
text:
My work generally falls into two main research areas:


Environmental DNA & Fisheries Ecology
I develop molecular and quantitative approaches for understanding marine communities and fisheries in the California Current Ecosystem. My research integrates quantitative eDNA, metabarcoding, community ecology, species co-occurrence, spatial modeling, and fisheries survey data to improve biodiversity monitoring, abundance estimation, and ecosystem-based management.


Hydrothermal Vent Ecology
I study the ecology and evolution of deep-sea hydrothermal vent ecosystems, with a particular focus on host–microbe symbioses, population genomics, microbial biogeography, and phage–microbe interactions. My work combines oceanographic field expeditions, molecular ecology, and bioinformatics to understand how organisms adapt and persist in one of Earth’s most extreme environments.

title: Michelle Hauer, Ph.D.
text:
About Me





I am a Postdoctoral Scholar jointly appointed at the University of Washington’s School of Marine and Environmental Affairs and NOAA’s Northwest Fisheries Science Center, where I develop molecular and quantitative approaches for marine conservation and fisheries science. I earned my Ph.D. in Oceanography from the University of Rhode Island’s Graduate School of Oceanography in 2024.
My research spans marine systems that are difficult to observe directly, from fish communities across the California Current Ecosystem to microbial symbioses at deep-sea hydrothermal vents. I am interested in how interactions among organisms shape ecological systems across scales, and how molecular tools can reveal the otherwise hidden biological patterns that underlie biodiversity, population structure, species distributions, and ecosystem dynamics.

title: Michelle Hauer, Ph.D.
text:
Research Themes




Environmental DNA & Fisheries Ecology
Marine ecosystems are vast, dynamic, and often impossible to observe directly. I am fascinated by the idea that organisms continually leave behind molecular traces that allow us to study biodiversity without capturing, disturbing, or even seeing the species themselves. Environmental DNA offers a non-invasive approach to understanding marine ecosystems while expanding the kinds of ecological questions we are able to ask.
My research combines quantitative eDNA, metabarcoding, community ecology, species co-occurrence, spatial modeling, and fisheries survey data to investigate marine biodiversity, estimate species abundance, and understand how ecological communities respond to environmental change.
Northern Anchovy, Credit: Shutterstock





Holobionts & Hydrothermal Vent Ecology
Hydrothermal vents provide a remarkable natural laboratory for exploring one of ecology’s most fundamental questions: how interactions among organisms give rise to increasingly complex biological systems. I am fascinated by the ways symbioses blur the boundaries of individuality, transforming animals, their microbial symbionts, and even the viruses that infect those symbionts into tightly integrated biological systems.
My research investigates hydrothermal vent holobionts across multiple levels of biological organization, from host–microbe and phage–microbe interactions to patterns of microbial biogeography and population connectivity. By combining oceanographic fieldwork with molecular ecology, population genomics, and bioinformatics, I seek to understand how evolutionary history, dispersal, and ecological interactions shape these nested biological systems in one of Earth’s most extreme environments.
Hydrothermal vent in the Lau Basin. Image credit: WHOI, ROV Jason.

title: Research (Under Construction)
text:
Environmental DNA & Fisheries Science

Environmental DNA (eDNA) provides a powerful, non-invasive approach for monitoring marine biodiversity and fisheries resources. Rather than relying exclusively on physically capturing organisms, eDNA detects genetic material continuously released into the environment, allowing species to be surveyed from seawater alone.
My current postdoctoral research at the University of Washington and NOAA develops quantitative eDNA methods for fisheries management and ecosystem monitoring. I integrate qPCR, metabarcoding, fisheries acoustics, oceanographic sampling, and statistical modeling to investigate fish abundance, species distributions, and community composition across the California Current Ecosystem.

Current focal species

Pacific hake (Merluccius productus)
Pacific sardine (Sardinops sagax)
Northern anchovy (Engraulis mordax)

title: Research (Under Construction)
text:
Hydrothermal Vent Holobionts

 Image: WHOI ROV Jason
 Image: Dexter Davis
 Image: WHOI ROV Jason

My doctoral research focused on deep-sea hydrothermal vent ecosystems, where animals depend on microbial symbionts rather than photosynthesis for survival.
I investigate hydrothermal vent holobionts across multiple levels of biological organization—from host–microbe symbioses to bacteriophage interactions and microbial population genomics—primarily in the vent gastropod genera Alviniconcha and Ifremeria and the vent mussel genus Bathymodiolus. hese systems provide natural laboratories for studying evolution, dispersal, adaptation, and the emergence of biological complexity. My research examines how environmental gradients, microbial interactions, environmental disturbance, and evolutionary history shape symbiont diversity, population connectivity, abundance, and function.

title: Research (Under Construction)
text:
Metabarcoding & Community Ecology

Beyond species-specific analyses, I use environmental DNA metabarcoding to characterize entire marine communities.
By combining high-throughput sequencing with ecological statistics, I investigate community composition, species co-occurrence, biodiversity change through time, and ecosystem responses to environmental variability. These approaches allow us to study ecological communities at scales that are otherwise impossible using traditional sampling methods.
Applications include:

Community composition
Species co-occurrence
Biodiversity monitoring
Ecosystem assessment
Time-series analyses

title: Michelle Hauer

One of the central goals of my research is transforming molecular observations into ecological inference.
I develop spatial and spatiotemporal statistical models that integrate environmental DNA with oceanographic variables and NOAA fisheries survey data to estimate species abundance and distribution.
